questions.text: The periodic time of a simple pendulum of length 1m & amplitude 2 cm is 5 sec. If the amplitude is made 4 cm, its periodic time in sec is:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Incorrect, to the explanation provided at the bottom of the page.

Initial amplitude= 2 cm = 0.02 m Initial time period = 5 seconds Now, let's calculate the new time period with an amplitude of 4 cm: New amplitude = 4 cm = 0.04 m The effective length of the pendulum with the new amplitude is given by: Effective Length = New amplitude x (1 - (1/16)) = 0.04 x (1 - 1/16) = 0.04 x (15/16) ≈ 0.0375 m Now, we can calculate the new time period with the effective length: New time period = 2π x √(L_eff / g) New time period = 2π x √(0.0375 / 9.81) New time period ≈ 2π x 0.0611 ≈ 0.384 seconds Option B is correct because the time period remains the same (5 seconds) when the amplitude is changed from 2 cm to 4 cm, assuming the small angle approximation holds true. The other options are incorrect because they do not match the calculated time period for the new amplitude.
Incorrect, to the explanation provided at the bottom of the page.
Incorrect, to the explanation provided at the bottom of the page.

questions.explanation: The value of 5 seconds matches the initial time period when the amplitude was 2 cm. Changing the amplitude to 4 cm doesn't affect the time period.

questions.text: The ratio of diameters of two wires of the same material is n : 1. The length of each wire is 4 m. On applying the same load, the increase in length of the thin wire will be (n > 1):
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Let's denote the diameter of the thicker wire as D and the diameter of the thinner wire as d. Since the ratio of diameters is n:1, we can write: D/d = n Now, the cross-sectional area of a wire is proportional to the square of its diameter (assuming the wires have the same shape): Area ∝ (diameter)2 So, the cross-sectional area of the thicker wire and the thinner wire are related as follows: A D / A d = (D2) / (d2) = (n2) When the same load is applied to both wires, they experience the same force. The tensile stress is defined as the applied force (F) divided by the cross-sectional area (A) of the wire: Stress = F / A Since the applied force is the same for both wires, we can write Stress D / Stress d = (F / A D) / (F / A d) = A d / A D = 1 / (n2) Now, the strain of a wire is directly proportional to the stress applied to it. So, the strain in the thinner wire and the strain in the thicker wire is related as follows: strain d / strain D = 1 / (n2) Now, the question asks for the increase in length of the thin wire compared to the thick wire. The increase in length is directly proportional to the strain: increase in length d / increase in length D = strain d / strain D = 1 / (n2) Since we know that the increase in length of the thicker wire is the same for both wires and is equal to 4m, we can write: increase in length d = (1 / (n2)) x increase in length D = (1 / (n2)) x 4m = 4m / n2 Therefore, the increase in length of the thin wire is n2 times smaller than the increase in length of the thick wire.
This is not correct. The increase in length of the thin wire is n^2 times smaller than the increase in length of the thick wire, not n times smaller.
This is not correct. There is no relation between the increase in length and 2n.
This is not correct. There is no relation between the increase in length and (2n + 1).

questions.explanation: Let's denote the diameter of the thicker wire as D and the diameter of the thinner wire as d. Since the ratio of diameters is n:1, we can write: D/d = n Now, the cross-sectional area of a wire is proportional to the square of its diameter (assuming the wires have the same shape): Area ∝ (diameter)^2 So, the cross-sectional area of the thicker wire and the thinner wire is related as follows: A D / A d = (D2) / (d2) = (n2) When the same load is applied to both wires, they experience the same force. The tensile stress is defined as the applied force (F) divided by the cross-sectional area (A) of the wire: Stress = F / A Since the applied force is the same for both wires, we can write Stress D / Stress d = (F / A D) / (F / A d) = A d / A D = 1 / (n2) Now, the strain of a wire is directly proportional to the stress applied to it. So, the strain in the thinner wire and the strain in the thicker wire is related as follows: strain d / strain D = 1 / (n2) Now, the question asks for the increase in length of the thin wire compared to the thick wire. The increase in length is directly proportional to the strain: increase in length d / increase in length D = strain d / strain D = 1 / (n2) Since we know that the increase in length of the thicker wire is the same for both wires and is equal to 4m, we can write: increase in length d = (1 / (n2)) x increase in length D = (1 / (n2)) x 4m = 4m / n2 Therefore, the increase in length of the thin wire is n2 times smaller than the increase in length of the thick wire.

questions.text: A 5 kg mass bounces in simple harmonic motion at the end of a spring. At which point is the acceleration of the mass the greatest?
questions.subject: Physics

options.text: When the spring is fully compressed and when the spring is fully extended
options.explanation: When the spring is fully compressed and when the spring is fully extended: When the mass is at the fully compressed position, it experiences maximum acceleration because the spring force is at its maximum, pulling the mass towards the equilibrium position. Similarly, at the fully extended position, the mass also experiences maximum acceleration due to the spring force pulling it back toward the equilibrium position.

options.text: When the spring is at its rest length
options.explanation: When the mass is at the rest length (equilibrium position), it experiences zero acceleration. At this point, the spring force is balanced by the inertial force of the mass, resulting in no net force and, therefore, no acceleration.

options.text: When the spring is halfway between its rest length and is fully extended or compressed length
options.explanation: The acceleration at this point is less than at the fully extended or compressed positions but greater than at the equilibrium position. The acceleration increases as the mass moves away from the equilibrium position (in either direction), reaching its maximum at the fully extended or compressed positions.
This option is incorrect. In simple harmonic motion, the acceleration is not constant but varies with the displacement from the equilibrium position, as described above.

questions.explanation: When the spring is fully compressed or fully extended when Hook's law forces are the greatest, and this indicates that the acceleration is the greatest. F = ma. Option A is correct because the acceleration is greatest when the spring is fully compressed and when it is fully extended. At the rest length, the acceleration is zero, and at the halfway point between rest and fully extended/compressed positions, the acceleration is less than the maximum value but not constant.

questions.text: A body moves with constant angular velocity on a circle. Magnitude of angular acceleration is:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This option is incorrect.
This option is incorrect.
This option is correct.
This option is incorrect.

questions.explanation: Explanation is given below. If a body moves with constant angular velocity on a circle, then its angular acceleration is zero. This is because acceleration is defined as the rate of change of velocity with respect to time. In circular motion, the velocity of the body is constantly changing as it moves around the circle, but the magnitude of the velocity remains constant. Therefore, the angular acceleration of the body is zero. Mathematically, we can express this as: Angular acceleration (α) = dω/dt where ω is the angular velocity of the body. Since the angular velocity is constant, the derivative of ω with respect to time is zero, so the angular acceleration is also zero.

questions.text: A wheel having a diameter of 3 m starts from rest and accelerates uniformly to as angular velocity of 210 r.p.m. in 5 seconds. Angular acceleration of the wheel is:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This option is correct. The angular velocity of the wheel (ω) in radians per second can be calculated using the formula: ω = (2πN)/60 where N is the rotational speed of the wheel in revolutions per minute (rpm). Initially, the wheel is at rest, so its initial angular velocity is zero. After 5 seconds, the final angular velocity of the wheel is: N = 210 rpm ω = (2π x 210)/60 = 22π/3 radians/second The angular acceleration (α) of the wheel can be calculated using the formula: α = (ωf - ωi)/t where ωf is the final angular velocity, ωi is the initial angular velocity, and t is the time interval. Substituting the given values, we get: α = (22π/3 - 0)/5 = 4.4 radians/second^2 Therefore, the angular acceleration of the wheel is 4.4 radians/second^2.
This option is incorrect.
This option is incorrect.
This option is incorrect.
